text: The surgeon mobilizes the ascending colon, removing the omentum from the hepatic flexure. A tattoo marks the location of the submucosal lipoma, visible as a fullness in the colon wall.

The surgeon makes an incision in the colon, ensuring not to compromise blood flow or narrow the lumen during closure. The incision is oriented to locate the submucosal lipoma, which is identified on the posterior wall. The surgeon uses a white load stapler to lift and staple the lipoma, similar to techniques used in a right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon excises the specimen and places it in the right upper quadrant. Two silk sutures are applied at both ends of the incision to aid in retraction and mark the ends of the colotomy for closure.

The surgeon uses silk sutures as markers for retraction, lifting the tissue. A Maryland dissector is passed to ensure the lumen remains open distally. The proximal end is marked, aiding in retraction. The surgeon closes the incision in two layers, demonstrating the technique for educational purposes.

The surgeon administers indocyanine green (ICG) dye to assess blood flow in the surgical field, confirming adequate perfusion. No drains are placed, and the procedure is straightforward, allowing for same-day discharge.
